top_categories.workout_context: 75ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
Race-pace efforts that mimic GFNY New York's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before May.
At GFNY New York, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
At mile 60 of 85, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
6,380ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of GFNY New York. Train it until it feels boring.
GFNY New York's paved Hudson Valley roads with sustained climbs forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 85 miles.
6,380ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on paved Hudson Valley roads with sustained climbs. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 6,380ft of climbing on paved Hudson Valley roads with sustained climbs does to your power file on race day.
How long can you hold 105% FTP before you blow? At GFNY New York, that number decides whether you bridge or get dropped.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 85 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for GFNY New York’s 85 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on TT Threshold, Race Simulation, and Over Under to prepare you for 85 miles of paved hudson valley roads with sustained climbs and rolling ridgeline terrain in Fort Lee, New Jersey / Hudson Valley, New York, USA.
